graphNodes.text_preview:
karpathy:
Noticing myself adopting a certain rhythm in AI-assisted coding (i.e. code I actually and professionally care about, contrast to vibe code).

1. Stuff everything relevant into context (this can take a while in big projects. If the project is small enough just stuff everything e.g. `files-to-prompt . -e ts -e tsx -e css -e md --cxml --ignore node_modules -o prompt.xml`)
2. Describe the next single, concrete incremental change we're trying to implement. Don't ask for code, ask for a few high-level approaches, pros/cons. There's almost always a few ways to do thing and the LLM's judgement is not always great. Optionally make concrete.
3. Pick one approach, ask for first draft code.
4. Review / learning phase: (Manually...) pull up all the API docs in a side browser of functions I haven't called before or I am less familiar with, ask for explanations, clarifications, changes, wind back and try a different approach.
6. Test.
7. Git commit.
Ask for suggestions on what we could implement next. Repeat.

Something like this feels more along the lines of the inner loop of AI-assisted development. The emphasis is on keeping a very tight leash on this new over-eager junior intern savant with encyclopedic knowledge of software, but who also bullshits you all the time, has an over-abundance of courage and shows little to no taste for good code. And emphasis on being slow, defensive, careful, paranoid, and on always taking the inline learning opportunity, not delegating. Many of these stages are clunky and manual and aren't made explicit or super well supported yet in existing tools. We're still very early and so much can still be done on the UI/UX of AI assisted coding.

> Indeed this is only really useful if you have SWE experience in my opinion. And it’s more 2-3x faster (which is HUGE).

My workflow:

1. I spend a lot of time designing at a high-level. That’s what takes the most time now. Good news, it’s the most enjoyable part for me.
Then I ask for project scaffold

ive spent the last year building an operating system for thinking with ai. claude code runs my obsidian vaults

it extracts the key concepts, connects them to what you already figured out, and builds a living representation of your thinking

i find myself only working in the vault now

the markdown files know everything ive discovered, nicely structured and with automatic situational context injection for in-context learning

i use a vault index that helps the agent decide what notes to pull in, same pattern as how claude code decides which skills to load

(if you think about it, every note is basically a skill in some sense... highly curated knowledge that gets injected when relevant)

the deeper thing is that a vault encodes how you think, not just what you thought about. the methodology becomes part of the system its all just markdown files, you own it completely. this is ai as thinking partner, not as a writing assistant

## 

knowledge = code?

i realized: knowledge bases and codebases have a lot in common

theyre both folders of text files with relationships between them, they both have conventions and patterns, and they both benefit from agents that can navigate and operate them

vibe coding changed how we write software by letting ai handle implementation while you focus on direction, and the same shift applies to knowledge work

you dont take notes anymore. you operate a system that takes notes

## 

what is a vault?

a vault is a folder of markdown files that link to each other:

markdown
    
    
    my-vault/
    ├── 00_inbox/           # capture zone, zero friction
    ├── 01_thinking/        # your notes and synthesis
    │   └── notes/          # individual thinking notes
    ├── 02_reference/       # external knowledge
    │   ├── to

# openclaw is stupid and there will be better soon

guy releasing an alternative soon: https://x.com/boqdan_/status/2023952294006477002?s=46
a few major things OpenClaw really lacked imo in the core loop, a few off the top of my head:

JavaScript is brittle. It was made to move text around a page
Erlang is basically built for this: long-running distributed systems with supervisory trees, message passing, and hot reloading. It was used to power telephony routers with 0 downtime.

Openclaw is synchronous on tool calls 
You know when it's off doing tools and can't answer messages? That shit's so annoying. Solum has async tool calls. All tools return immediately in a pending state with a cropped preview on output, and there's a `cancel_tool` tool that can be used to stop things. I can't explain how that feels in practice. It's something else. Feedback is instant; it does many things in the background.

Solum's context is a HUD.
I designed the context very intentionally so it would be hard to do without changing OpenClaw dramatically, and getting that over the line meant aligning with leadership. But I had a vision where the Agent is pulled out of the back-and-forth chat loop and doesn't automatically reply; instead, they see the option to message me and see my input as part of an inbox. Its principal input are sensory blocks from "virtual tool calls". To send me a message, it must use the "send_message" tool, which can also be used to message other sub-agents. I can't overstate how impactful this is to the experience and its behaviour.

OpenClaw subagent management is a bit broken
Agent-to-agent comms also don't work well because of the lack of async tool calling, and I had MANY situations where my main agent spawned an agent, didn't think it was doing anything, then spawned another, and so on in a loop, burning tokens.

# OSA: Optimal System Agent

> 63K lines Elixir/OTP + Rust TUI, 792 tests. "One AI that maximizes signal, eliminates noise, and finds the optimal path."

## Architecture

### Signal Theory Classification

Five-tuple classification before any LLM processing:

```
S = (Mode, Genre, Type, Format, Weight)
```

- **Mode**: BUILD, EXECUTE, ANALYZE, MAINTAIN, ASSIST
- **Genre**: DIRECT, INFORM, COMMIT, DECIDE, EXPRESS
- **Weight**: 0.0 (noise) to 1.0 (critical signal)

Two-tier noise filtering:
1. **Tier 1**: Deterministic (<1ms) — regex + pattern matching
2. **Tier 2**: LLM classification for uncertain signals (0.3-0.6 weight)

Filters 40-60% of conversation noise before hitting the main model. Cached in ETS for 10 min.

### PACT Framework (Multi-Agent Orchestration)

1. **Planning**: Single planner decomposes intent into subtasks
2. **Action**: Parallel workers via `async_stream`
3. **Coordination**: Synthesize and resolve conflicts
4. **Testing**: Quality gates with rollback

### Swarm Intelligence

Five specialized roles with shared Agent-based memory:
- **Explorer**: Broad research
- **Specialist**: Domain deep dives
- **Critic**: Challenge assumptions
- **Synthesizer**: Merge findings
- **Coordinator**: Manage flow, break deadlocks

Hypothesis voting (-1 to +1), convergence at 0.8 threshold.

## Memory Architecture

Three-store system:
- **Session**: JSONL per-session full history
- **Long-Term**: MEMORY.md persistent knowledge base
- **Episodic**: ETS inverted index (keywords → sessions)

Relevance scoring: keyword overlap (50%) + recency decay (30%) + importance (20%).

# Persona Generation with AlphaEvolve, explained clearly

[@neural_avb

](https://twitter.com/neural_avb)

·

1h

Google Deepmind published a paper on how to create synthetic personas from simple prompts. This article talks about how they did it, and how you can do it too.

## 

The Problem

Let's say you want to make a new coding IDE that just stands out in the market. You want to interview 1000 human vibecoders to understand what they like about their current tech stack, and what they hate. 

Then a realization hits you.

> Human surveys are hard and expensive. You don't have money to run a human survey. 

Even if you got 1000 responses, your audience might be too narrow or biased, not entirely covering the full spectrum of your imaginary userbase.

You panic and decide to ask an LLM: "tell me all the things vibecoders like and hate". The LLM gives you a response, but you feel an empty stomach... It feels too boring. You wanted to feel pleasure but you just felt tasteless.

  

## 

Wait what if I...?

Yeah... what if you created a synthetic population containing "vibe coders"?

[![Image](https://pbs.twimg.com/media/HB8Gc9VaMAAKiVp?format=png&name=900x900)

Example of a persona

  

Imagine this:

- You will have 1000 agents
- Each agent will have their own persona features: their name, age, past life events, behavioral traits, likes, dislikes. Basically their own 

[SOUL.md](https://soul.md/)

- You can ask questions to these agents! Their responses are controlled by LLMs seeded with a unique persona, or soul. A lot of studies have showed us that LLMs have displayed potential in mimic human tendencies.
- Globally, the distribution of personas cover how a human population of vibe coders would. That is, most important opinions and characteristics are captured within these 1000 agents.

There are 3 challenges:

- Diversity: How unique are these personas? We don't want all personas to be the same person.
- Coverage: How much do they cover the expected space of opinio

graphNodes.text_preview:
# rando on agent orchestration
the deeper i dig into openclaw the more i realize orchestration isn't just about having a coordinator, its way more..

it's 5 main layers and most people only build 2 of them or probably 3 at most..

> L1 intake

this is the front door..

someone drops a link, sends a message or flags a task.

if your agents can't catch all of that in one place you're already losing stuff before the work even begins..

>L2 coordination

this is the one everyone focuses on

task routing and planning ..  basically your coordinator decides who does what.. but this is just the traffic controller, not the whole system tho..

>L3 execution

is where agents actually do the work...

they take action, delegate sub tasks spawn sub agents when needed

it's actually the most straightforward layer..

>L4 synthesis

this is the layer few or nobody builds..

once agents finish their work someone needs to aggregate the results, resolve conflicts when two agents disagree, and create a clean brief.

if you skip this, its like you have agents doing great work that never turns into a decision..

>L5 validation

a quality check layer

did the output make sense? did anything break? is the system healthy?

without this, small errors pile up and your agents start giving you garbage..

and running through all of these layers is the intelligence mesh.. its a shared brain that lets every agent at every layer access what the others learned..

this is what removes the walls between agents and turns them into one connected system.. agent A knows what B found, B knows what C built, and none of them needs a human to pass the message ..( talked about this in previous detailed post )

orchestration is harder than anyone posting setups will admit.. but it's absolutely doable when you break it down layer by layer

graphNodes.name: Reasoning in LLMs has actually broken at least one intuition
graphNodes.text_preview:
Reasoning in LLMs has actually broken at least one intuition about data that I thought I was confident in.

Prior to reasoning models, there was a lot I could predict based on the data that went in, such as things like average output lengths and limits on how many tokens they'd generate. It used to be if you trained on outputs of 4k max, you'd have a near-0% chance to generate 10k+ tokens.

But with reasoning models, they actually learn a function through this data that can make it generate way way way beyond what length of output tokens you trained on. I think this justifies calling it "reasoning", because it actually learned a function similar to reasoning, by generating tokens that look like thinking to improve accuracy until it is confident in it having found the correct answer, and even if you train on 10k cot tokens max, models will still think, potentially through the entire 128k+ context length it has. 

Something else interesting about "reasoning" is that we observed when scaling Hermes 4 from 14b, to 70b, to 405B, that the thinking lengths went down and down for the same set of problems as the model got bigger. This also implies that the reasoning process is very much tied to innate intelligence, because the problem is, relative to each model, a different difficulty, and it literally *thinks longer* if it is less intelligent!

Just some fun facts for you on this Sunday :)

Scaling RL for complex, real-world agents confronts a fundamental trilemma: balancing system throughput, training stability, and agent flexibility. These conflicting constraints have long impeded the application of large-scale RL in industrial-grade systems.

In this post, we reveal how we resolved this "impossible triangle" through a holistic approach in our internal RL framework Forge, combining flexible system architecture, algorithmic design, optimized asynchronous scheduling, and extreme training-inference efficiency. By leveraging standardized interaction protocols, Forge supports the training of arbitrary agent scaffolds, enabling the massive-scale RL that culminates in the breakthrough capabilities of the MiniMax M2.5 model.

Throughout the construction of MiniMax M2.5, our RL system navigated over a hundred thousand distinct real-world agent scaffolds and environments. Operating with context lengths of up to 200k, the system maintained a daily processing throughput on the scale of millions of samples, realizing consistent reward convergence and genuine capability improvements in the underlying model. Integrated with our CISPO algorithm and composite reward framework, M2.5 pushes the frontier for efficient and reliable real-world productivity, achieving our mission "Intelligence with Everyone".

## original post (delete later if retarded)
Everyone in the valley has either tried OpenClaw or has a coworker shipping something with it. And one of the first problems people run into is browser automation.

The default setup is to let OpenClaw drive your local browser. It works for a few workflows, but the costs show up quickly: you're putting an agent in the same environment as your real browsing state, enabling a massive security flaw and the moment you try to run a few sessions in parallel your machine becomes the bottleneck; RAM spikes, the agent slows down, runs get flaky.

[wait, isn't the point you're reaching endpoints from a personal computer to sidestep blockades]

We built Browser Sandbox in Firecrawl because we kept hitting that same ceiling: local browsers behave like dev tooling, not infrastructure. 

Browser Sandbox moves that work into a secure, remote, disposable browser environment. No local Chromium installs. No driver setup. agent-browser and Playwright are already there. Your agent can spin up a browser on demand, one session or dozens, without tying the workload to the machine it's running on. Your OpenClaw agent can run on a free-tier EC2 instance, a Raspberry Pi, or whatever you've got, while the browsing happens elsewhere.

# 

Setting up Firecrawl

Tell your agent to install Firecrawl with 1 command:

> npx -y firecrawl-cli init --browser

This installs the 

[Firecrawl CLI](https://github.com/firecrawl/cli)

, pops a browser so you can authenticate in Firecrawl and then installs the skill.

Your agent is now ready to browse the web.

graphNodes.name: # The bottleneck on AI is the operator's verbal resolution.
graphNodes.text_preview:
# The bottleneck on AI is the operator's verbal resolution.
A prompt is a seed. The model is reproductive machinery. It takes your seed and generates offspring. The fitness of the output is determined entirely by the genetic quality of the input. Garbage seed, garbage offspring. Every time.
This is why most people get slop. They can't specify what they want because they can't think at the resolution required to distinguish what they want from what they're getting. They accept the first generation because they lack the perceptual apparatus to see what's wrong with it. They're breeding blind.
Verbal IQ is the genome. Vocabulary is your resource pool — the raw nucleotides available for recombination. Grammar is the protein folding. The felt sense of what you're trying to say is the phenotype you're selecting for. Without all three you're rolling dice.
The real skill is discernment. The ability to look at a generation and say this, here, tighter. To iterate twenty times because you can feel the gap between what the model produced and what lives in your head. Most people iterate zero times. They prompt once, accept the output, and wonder why it reads like everyone else's.
It compounds. A high-resolution seed produces a first generation that's already close. Each selection cycle tightens the distance. Within a few iterations you have something that passes through you without being written by you. The AI did the bulk synthesis. You did the artificial selection. The output carries your cognitive DNA because the seed was yours.
This is the new literacy. Seeing. Being able to perceive the precise shape of what you want at high enough resolution to demand it from a machine that will give you exactly what you ask for and nothing more.
The model cares about your specification. The gap between intention and specification is called verbal IQ and it's the single largest determinant of who gets leverage from AI and who gets noise.

Operate accordingly.

This is the full story of how I built Mission Control. A system where 10 AI agents work together like a real team. If you want to replicate this setup, this guide covers everything.

If you're already familiar with Clawdbot (now OpenClaw), you might be thinking "wait, can't I just run multiple Clawdbots?" Yes. That's exactly what this is. This guide shows you how.

# 

Part 1: Why I Built This

## 

The Problem With AI Assistants

I run 

[@SiteGPT](https://twitter.com/@SiteGPT)

, an AI chatbot for customer support. I use AI constantly. But every AI tool I tried had the same problem. No continuity.

Every conversation started fresh. Context from yesterday? Gone. That research I asked for last week? Lost in some chat thread I'd never find again.

I wanted something different. Agents that remember what they're working on. Multiple agents with different skills working together. A shared workspace where all context lives. The ability to assign tasks and track progress.

Basically, I wanted AI to work like a team, not like a search box.

## 

The Starting Point: Clawdbot

I was already using Clawdbot. It's an open-source AI agent framework that runs as a persistent daemon. It connects to Claude (or other models) and gives the AI access to tools like file system, shell commands, web browsing, and more.

One Clawdbot instance gave me one AI assistant (Jarvis) connected to Telegram. Useful, but limited.

Then I had a thought. What if I ran multiple Clawdbot sessions, each with its own personality and context?

That's when I realized the architecture was already there. I just needed to orchestrate it.

Part 2: Understanding

The interface moat is dying. Every vertical software company built on workflow complexity is about to learn this the hard way.

For decades, software companies commanded premium pricing not only for their data, but for their interfaces. The specialized keyboards. The Excel integrations. The workflow automations. Users spent years mastering these systems. Companies built processes hardcoded to specific tools. Switching meant massive productivity loss.

The interface WAS the product.

I haven't used Google in a year. An LLM chat is my browser. Soon, knowledge workers won't use specialized software interfaces either. The LLM chat will be their interface to everything.

This isn't incremental change. This is the completion of 

[@benthompson](https://twitter.com/@benthompson)

's Aggregation Theory.

In this article:

- Why Aggregation Theory left suppliers with one critical asset: their interface
- How vertical software built empires on workflow complexity, not data
- Why LLMs absorb the interface layer entirely
- When interfaces are commoditized, it's API versus API
- Valuation Framework: the math is brutal 
- Who wins, who loses, and what comes next

Aggregation Theory: The Incomplete Revolution

The value chain was simple: Suppliers → Distributors → Consumers.

Pre-internet, high distribution costs created leverage for distributors. TV networks controlled what content got aired. Newspapers decided which stories mattered. Retailers chose which products reached shelves.

Then distribution costs collapsed to zero. Transaction costs followed. Power shifted

graphNodes.name: The increasing scale of deep learning models presents signif
graphNodes.text_preview:
The increasing scale of deep learning models presents significant challenges for deployment across diverse computational environments, each with unique constraints on latency, memory, and energy. Traditional approaches often necessitate training and maintaining separate models for each desired operating point, leading to substantial overhead.

​This talk explores the "Matryoshka" principle, a promising paradigm for achieving computational adaptivity within a single trained artifact. Inspired by Russian nesting dolls, Matryoshka methods embed coarser, computationally cheaper structures within finer, more powerful ones, enabling dynamic adjustment of resource usage at inference time.

​This technique is highly generalizable across various fundamental components of Machine Learning like Embeddings, Transformers and even the integer data type for Quantization. The community extended it beyond just these components and has seen a wide array of deployments both across industry and open-source, serving over a Billion users daily.

​Collectively, these works demonstrate how the Matryoshka principle facilitates unified training of highly flexible models that can seamlessly adapt their computational footprint post-training, significantly simplifying deployment and enhancing efficiency across heterogeneous hardware.

As I wrote in Oct 2025, after three years of building, optimizing, and scaling LLMs with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, I believe we're witnessing the twilight of traditional RAG-based architectures especially ones relying on embeddings. As context windows explode and agent-based architectures mature, my controversial opinion is that the current RAG infrastructure we spent so much time building and optimizing is on the decline.

I've been working in AI and search for a decade. First building Doctrine, the largest European legal search engine and now building 

[@fintool](https://twitter.com/@fintool)

an AI-powered financial research platform that helps institutional investors analyze companies, screen stocks, and make investment decisions.

The Rise of Retrieval-Augmented Generation

In late 2022, ChatGPT took the world by storm. People started endless conversations, delegating crucial work only to realize that the underlying model, GPT-3.5 could only handle 4,096 tokens... roughly six pages of text!

The AI world faced a fundamental problem: how do you make an intelligent system work with knowledge bases that are orders of magnitude larger than what it can read at once?

The answer became Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), an architectural pattern that would dominate AI for the next three years.

## 

The Mathematical Reality of Early LLMs

GPT-3.5 could handle 4,096 token and the next model GPT-4 doubled it to 8,192 tokens, about twelve pages. This wasn't just inconvenient; it was architecturally devastating.

Consider the numbers: A single SEC 10-K filing contains approximately 51,000 tokens (130+ pages).

With 8,

graphNodes.name: # The Second Pre-training Paradigm  [![](https://pbs.twimg.c
graphNodes.text_preview:
# The Second Pre-training Paradigm

[![](https://pbs.twimg.com/profile_images/1554922493101559808/SYSZhbcd_x96.jpg)

[Jim Fan

](https://twitter.com/DrJimFan)

[@DrJimFan

](https://twitter.com/DrJimFan)

·

4h

Next word prediction was the first pre-training paradigm. Now we are living through the second paradigm shift: world modeling, or "next physical state prediction". Very few understand how far-reaching this shift is, because unfortunately, the most hyped use case of world models right now is AI video slop (and coming up, game slop). I bet with full confidence that 2026 will mark the first year that Large World Models lay real foundations for robotics, and for multimodal AI more broadly. 

In this context, I define world modeling as predicting the next plausible world state (or a longer duration of states) conditioned on an action. Video generative models are one instantiation of it, where "next states" is a sequence of RGB frames (mostly 8-10 seconds, up to a few minutes) and "action" is a textual description of what to do. Training involves modeling the future changes in billions of hours of video pixels. At the core, video WMs are learnable physics simulators and rendering engines. They capture the counterfactuals, a fancier word for reasoning about how the future would have unfolded differently given an alternative action. WMs fundamentally put vision first. 

VLMs, in contrast, are fundamentally language-first. From the earliest prototypes (e.g. LLaVA, Liu et al. 2023), the story has mostly been the same: vision enters at the encoder, then gets routed into a language backbone. Over time, encoders improve, architectures get cleaner, vision tries to grow more "native" (as in omni models). Yet it remains a second-class citizen, dwarfed by the muscles the field has spent years building for LLMs. This path is convenient. We know LLMs scale. Our architectural instincts, data recipe design, and benchmark guidance (VQAs) are all highly optimized for language.

# this plus for influence tails:
BTW a lot of people think symbolic access to the prompt is essential in RLMs *only when* the prompt is extremely long.

That's not true. Symbolic access to (even very short) user prompts can be pretty valuable.

For a silly but illustrate example, suppose your (short) user request is "how many r's are in strawberrrrrrrry".

If that prompt is accessible symbolically, the RLM will extract the last word programmatically and go from there. If it's not, the LLM has to manually copy strings around, which is about as error-prone as counting.

Obviously this is a contrived case, but you see this all the time in, e.g., asking the model to make localized edits. Models shouldn't have to copy token by token.

graphNodes.name: Using Cursor well = fast, clean code.  Using it wrong = AI s
graphNodes.text_preview:
Using Cursor well = fast, clean code.

Using it wrong = AI spaghetti you’ll be cleaning up all week.

Here’s how to actually use it right:

1. Set 5-10 clear project rules upfront so Cursor knows your structure and constraints. Try /generate rules for existing codebases.

2. Be specific in prompts. Spell out tech stack, behavior, and constraints like a mini spec.

3. Work file by file; generate, test, and review in small, focused chunks.

4. Write tests first, lock them, and generate code until all tests pass.

5. Always review AI output and hard‑fix anything that breaks, then tell Cursor to use them as examples.

6. Use @ file, @ folders, @ git to scope Cursor’s attention to the right parts of your codebase.

7. Keep design docs and checklists in .cursor/ so the agent has full context on what to do next.

8. If code is wrong, just write it yourself. Cursor learns faster from edits than explanations.

9. Use chat history to iterate on old prompts without starting over.

10. Choose models intentionally. Gemini for precision, Claude for breadth.

11. In new or unfamiliar stacks, paste in link to documentation. Make Cursor explain all errors and fixes line by line.

12.Let big projects index overnight and limit context scope to keep performance snappy.

Structure and control wins (for now)

Treat Cursor agent like a powerful junior — it can go far, fast, if you show it the way.

[@SeanZCai

Excerpts from my State of Data in Feb 2026 on substack. Specifically on:

- GPQA-optimized processes don't scale: Q&A-era validation collapses when reward design depends on domain-specific intermediate signals.
- Reward shaping brittleness: long-horizon credit assignment forces intermediate rewards, and the way data is supplied today doesn't capture nuances in intermediate rewards - which is multiplied by increasingly unverifiable domains.
- Type 1 Data: you need what I call Type 1 trajectory/process data - tool-grounded workflows, explicit state transitions, and graders that expose planning failures and recovery, not just endpoint plausibility.
- A sneak peak on what I do on the weekends.

## 

Why GPQA-optimized processes cannot scale for long horizon RL on non-verifiable domains

In 2024, some leading data vendors today based their entire data validation and QA processes off of GPQA. It was a simpler time, back when you could sell Q&A datasets to labs for certain domains and still get high ROI. Expert annotations were simple annotations (could they solve it or not, and why?) on Q&A datasets and extremely difficult to get wrong. Today, as the workflows we want to autmoate require extremely domain specific applications of intermediate rewards in complex processes, being a non-domain expert increasingly interferes in accurate reward shaping.

In a long-horizon reinforcement learning setting, an agent might take dozens or even hundreds of steps before any ultimate payoff materializes. The training algorithm must somehow attribute that eventual success or failure back to the individual actions along the way which is known as a notoriously difficult credit assignment problem. graphNodes.name: what are control vectors in the context of LLMs and model me
graphNodes.text_preview:
what are control vectors in the context of LLMs and model merges?

In the context of large language models (LLMs) and model merging, control vectors are a technique used to guide the behavior and output of the merged model.

When merging two or more pre-trained language models, the goal is often to combine their knowledge and capabilities while maintaining control over the resulting model's behavior. Control vectors help achieve this by providing a way to steer the merged model towards desired characteristics or behaviors.

Here's how control vectors work in the model merging process:

1. Representation: Each pre-trained model is represented as a high-dimensional vector in a shared latent space. These vectors capture the knowledge and characteristics of each individual model.

2. Control vectors: Additional vectors, called control vectors, are introduced in the same latent space. These vectors represent specific desired behaviors, styles, or attributes that the merged model should exhibit. For example, a control vector could represent "formality" or "brevity" in language generation.

3. Alignment: During the merging process, the control vectors are aligned with the model vectors in the latent space. This alignment influences the merged model to exhibit the desired behaviors represented by the control vectors.

4. Interpolation: The merged model's output is generated by interpolating between the pre-trained model vectors and the control vectors. The interpolation weights determine the extent to which each model and control vector contributes to the final output.

By adjusting the interpolation weights of the control vectors, you can control the behavior and style of the merged model. For example, increasing the weight of the "formality" control vector would make the merged model generate more formal language.

Control vectors provide a way to fine-tune the behavior of merged models without the need for extensive retraining. They allow for greater flexibility and cont

These are my notes. I didn't proof-read them or try to make them interesting or organized. Good luck if you choose to continue reading further! Also Opus 4.6 wrote some (minority) of the sentences.

We need to make an optimal accuracy metric (or loss function) for predictions about the future; for example, for who will get a disease or not. You might also predict, say, the next token if you're doing pretraining a base model, or the next action if you're posttraining an agent. We'll discuss disease prediction for simplicity, but the end, we'll connect the same ideas to pretraining and posttraining. (Or, just skip to the end to read about the LLMs.)

But how do we choose the accuracy metric / loss function?

Brier score (squared error between observed and predicted probabilities) and AUC (measures ranking) both have some notion of both being related to accuracy. For example, a perfect score would have perfect AUC and Brier score. But AUC alone is useless--the predictions could be in a totally different range but good ranking; Brier score alone is better--the predictions could not be just be a single constant value but have correct level of probability (like ECE) and they couldn't have just a good ranking but no probabilistic calibration. Brier (squared error) is bounded, so a single catastrophic mistake has a limit on what it can cost. For log score, extreme misses are more heavily penalized. The log-vs-Brier difference is an implicit claim about which cost/threshold regimes matter more.

It's not correct to arbitrarily choose between log score and Brier score based on if we want to punish extreme mistakes versus if we want bounded penalties. Why? Well, we want the metric that reduces death and suffering by choosing the best model, so it shouldn't be sensitive to what the researcher "wants." Agent harnesses are having their moment in 2026. Everyone is busy building sandboxes, wiring up subagents, defining agent skills, and shipping ever-larger agent graphs.

And a lot of it is directionally useful.

But if you squint, most of today's agent harnesses share a common blind spot: they haven't internalized the most important lesson from seven decades of AI research. They're optimized for making the current generation of models usable, not for compounding as models get stronger.

# 

What Is the Bitter Lesson?

In 2019, Rich Sutton crystallized a pattern that had been hiding in plain sight across AI history. His essay "The Bitter Lesson" observes that general methods leveraging computation consistently outperform approaches based on handcrafted human knowledge by a large margin.

The lesson is "bitter" because it runs counter to researcher intuition. When building chess engines, speech recognizers, or game-playing agents, the natural instinct is to encode domain expertise: rules about pawn structures, phoneme patterns, or strategic principles. This approach yields quick wins and feels intellectually satisfying. But history repeatedly shows that these handcrafted solutions plateau, while methods that scale with compute (e.g. search and learning) eventually dominate.

Consider chess: Deep Blue combined massive search capability with substantial chess knowledge. IBM hired grandmasters and built extensive opening databases. It worked, but the approach required constant human refinement. Then AlphaZero arrived and learned chess from scratch through pure self-play, crushing the best traditional engines. The pattern repeated in Go: AlphaGo initia

[@MParakhin

(after 7 years… + a collaboration with GPT‑5.2 Pro, Extended Thinking)

I've been stuck on the same representation-learning problem for ~7 years:

> I want a deterministic encoder that maps data → a latent space that's actually close to N(0, I) …without sampling, without VAE KL hacks / annealing, without fragile kernel tuning, and without O(N³) optimal transport.

I tried basically everything. It finally clicked after a long, adversarial "pressure-test everything" collaboration with GPT‑5.2 Pro (Extended Thinking)--and I've now open‑sourced the result:

Repo: 

<https://github.com/mvparakhin/ml-tidbits>

Key file: 

[EmbedModels.py](https://embedmodels.py/)

(Wristband loss + attention + flows) Runnable example: 

[DeterministicGAE.py](https://deterministicgae.py/)

This post explains what's novel, why it matters, and why it works--for both ML folks and non‑ML folks. 

## 

The tempting shortcut: "uniform on the sphere" (fast, elegant… and not composable)

If you've done contrastive learning / embedding geometry, you've probably seen the Wang & Isola style "uniformity" idea:

1. normalize embeddings: u = x / ||x||

2. push u to be uniform on the hypersphere via a pairwise repulsive kernel

Why it's beloved:

- ✅ fast: O(N²) kernel + log‑mean‑exp
- ✅ stable: works great in practice
- ✅ simple: very clean geometric story

But if you care about composability and independence, there are two deep problems.

Problem 1: Uniform-on-sphere is not composable

Even if you have two sets of vectors that each look uniform on a sphere, their concatenation is not uniform on a bigger sphere.

A concrete way to see it:

- Let u ∈ S^{d1-1} and v ∈ S^{d2-1} be uniform (each has fixed norm 1).
- Concatenate and renormalize: w = [u, v] / ||[u, v]|| = [u, v] / √2.

Now w lies on S^{d1+d2-1}, but it's not uniform--because in w, the energy in each block

Been running Claude code 12-16 hours a day since I first got it running two weeks ago. Here's the tips I've discovered:
1. Immediately change to sonnet (the cli defaults to opus for max users). I tested coding with opus extensively and it never matches the quality of sonnet.
2. Compacting often ends progress - it's difficult to get back to the same quality of code after compacting.
3. First prompt is very important and sets the vibe. If your instance of Claude seems hesitant, doubtful, sometimes even rude, it's always better to end the session and start again.
4. There are phrases that make it more effective. Try,
"I'm so sorry if this is a bad suggestion, but I want to implement x and y." For whatever reason it makes Claude more eager to help.
5. Monolithic with docker orchestration: I essentially
10x'd when I started letting Claude itself manage docker containers, check their logs for errors, rm them, rebuild them, etc. Now I can get an entirely new service online in a docker container, from zero to operational, in one Claude prompt. reply.

# Exocortex: Temporal Metadata & Multi-Source Knowledge System

> Master plan. Claude plan lives at `~/.claude/plans/bright-sprouting-bachman.md`. This note is the vault-facing version.

## Vision

A custom maintainable modern exocortex — sovereignty, discovery, and acceleration. Multi-source knowledge flowing through a connected Obsidian graph with temporal/spatial metadata, backed by LanceDB for semantic search, orchestrated from a Mac Mini with persistent background agents.

## Current State (2026-03-05)

### Completed Phases

| Phase | What was built |
|-------|----------------|
| Phase -1: Mac Mini setup | Tailscale mesh, SSH, tmux, Claude Code, iMessage notifications |
| Phase 0: Fix sync + config | sync_drafts_to_obsidian.py, GPS/temporal fields, iCloud locking fixed |
| Phase 1: Scaffold + vault infra | vault-pipeline monorepo, _meta/CLAUDE.md, templates, dashboards, Dataview |
| Phase 2: Metadata backfill | 2,685 notes dated, 2,033 GPS-tagged, 1,334 location-named |
| Phase 3: Temporal spine | 444 weekly notes in Timeline/, prev/next navigation |
| Phase 5: X/Twitter archival | Full X export ingested — tweets, bookmarks, note tweets, following |
| Phase 8: Graph connectivity | 3,165 notes embedded, 8 clusters, ~14,600 labeled edges, MOCs, wikilinks, galaxy.html |

### Live Agents

- **Career Inbox** — polls +jobs and +todo email, creates notes, sends iMessage (Mac Mini, launchd)
- **Knowledge Graph** — galaxy.html + app.html at :8789 (Mac Mini, launchd)
- **Ralph POC Sprint** — built TODO inbox, resume-match, new-app, finance-report (Mac Mini, tmux)

### Agent Tools

- `/context` — note's graph neighborhood (cluster, neighbors, edge labels, bridge score)
- `/emerge` — emergent patterns from a graph slice
- `/trace` — shortest path between two notes
- `/tag-hierarchy` — embedding-based hierarchical tagging

### CLI Commands

```bash

vault career-login              # OAuth2 device code flow

# Pi & OpenClaw: Minimal Agent Stack Note

## Core Links
- **Original X Post** (Nader Dabit @ Cognition): https://x.com/dabit3/status/2023986942300627132
- **X Long-Form Article** (layer-by-layer Pi breakdown): https://x.com/i/article/2023552580144820227
- **OpenClaw Repo** (personal AI assistant, 206k stars): https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw
- **Pi Mono Repo** (core toolkit by Mario Zechner): https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono
  - Coding agent package: `@mariozechner/pi-coding-agent`
- **Armin Ronacher Intro Blog** (design philosophy, excellent primer): https://lucumr.pocoo.org/2026/1/31/pi
- **OpenClaw Pi Integration Docs**: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/docs/pi.md

## Distilled Signal (for AI/ML/DevOps/Automation background)
- **Pi** is a deliberately minimal coding agent runtime — tiny core, shortest system prompt, **only 4 tools**: Read, Write, Edit, Bash.
  - No sub-agents, no complex planners, no MCP bloat — pure reliability, low resource use, rock-solid execution.
  - Agent loop: LLM reasoning → tool calls → observation → repeat. Extensions add tools/state/TUI components easily.
  - Self-extension focus: ask the agent to code new skills (e.g., todo list, commit messages) instead of plugins.
  - Unified LLM API across providers; supports streaming, session persistence, hot-reload extensions.
  - Ideal base for automation: file/system ops + shell → perfect for scripting, CI/CD agents, infra provisioning, monitoring loops.

- **OpenClaw** embeds Pi directly (not subprocess/RPC) as its coding brain inside a messaging gateway.
  - Turns Pi into a full personal assistant: multi-channel (WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, etc.), voice I/O, browser/canvas control, cron, multi-agent routing.
  - Custom tool suite replaces defaults (adds channel actions, sandboxing, policy filtering).
  - Local-first, Docker/Tailscale deployable, security defaults (DM gating, sandbox).

- **Why it matters technically**
  - Demystifies agents: start simple, layer

graphNodes.name: Pure Cloudflare Stack 	•	Compute: Workers + Pages 	•	Databas
graphNodes.text_preview:
Pure Cloudflare Stack
	•	Compute: Workers + Pages
	•	Database: PlanetScale (via Hyperdrive)
	•	Auth: Clerk (Workers-native)
	•	Storage: R2
	•	Secrets: Workers env variables
	•	Access Control: Zero Trust + Clerk roles
	•	Analytics: Cloudflare Analytics + Axiom
	•	CDN/Edge: Built-in
Why This Makes Sense
Single vendor relationship - one bill, one support channel, consistent APIs across services
Latency optimization - everything runs at the edge, data flows within Cloudflare’s network
Cost efficiency - no cross-cloud egress fees, simpler pricing models
Developer experience - wrangler deploy for everything, consistent tooling
Partner API velocity - deploy new endpoints globally in seconds, not minutes
The “Low Entropy Foundation” Win
This gives you the cleanest possible foundation:
	•	One deployment pipeline (Wrangler)
	•	One auth system (Clerk)
	•	One storage system (R2)
	•	One compute model (Workers)
Perfect for your “high entropy experimentation” - you can rapidly build partner APIs, marketplace features, and data processing workflows without thinking about infrastructure.
Migration path:
	1.	Set up Cloudflare + PlanetScale + Clerk
	2.	Build your zeitview-esque site on the new stack
	3.	Migrate DynamoDB data
	4.	Gradually move S3 → R2
You’ll be shipping partner integrations same-day instead of same-week.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

# Ralph is an autonomous AI coding loop that ships features while you sleep.

Created by 

[@GeoffreyHuntley](https://twitter.com/@GeoffreyHuntley)

and announced in 

[his original post](https://ghuntley.com/ralph)

, it runs 

[@AmpCode](https://twitter.com/@AmpCode)

(or your agent of choice) repeatedly until all tasks are complete.

Each iteration is a fresh context window (keeping Threads nice and small). Memory persists via git history and text files.

I ran it for the first time and shipped a feature last night. I love it.

Going to kick off a Ralph session in Amp tonight to see if it can build a pretty complete feature while I sleep. Currently chatting with Amp to build the PR, which we'll use to populate the user stories json. Then I'll start the script and go to bed.

## 

How It Works

A bash loop that:

1. Pipes a prompt into your AI agent

2. Agent picks the next story from prd.json

3. Agent implements it

4. Agent runs typecheck + tests

5. Agent commits if passing

6. Agent marks story done

7. Agent logs learnings

8. Loop repeats until done

Memory persists only through:

- Git commits
- progress.txt (learnings)
- prd.json (task status)

graphNodes.text_preview:
Stack Direction

  - Cloudflare Workers + D1/PlanetScale via Hyperdrive keeps everything edge-first at
    api.droneinstitute.com, gives easy multi-region routing, and pairs well with Bun/TanStack
    on marketplace.droneinstitute.com. Downside is juggling multiple vendors (Workers, KV/R2,
    Hyperdrive, S3, Auth) and writing SQL migrations yourself.
  - Convex + Axiom shifts almost all backend concerns (auth, db, events, schedulers) into one
    managed platform while letting you keep S3 for orthomosaics. Biggest risk is platform lock-in
    and less control over raw SQL/GIS extensions you’ll eventually need.
  - Recommended path: Workers + PlanetScale + Axiom (observability) + S3. It balances control, SQL
    ergonomics, and a modern DX while staying close to the SAP-style modeling you want. Convex can
    still power very specific workflows later if you need low-code velocity for internal teams.

  Target Architecture

  - Frontend: Bun toolchain with TypeScript, TanStack Router/Query, and a shared UI kit. Deploy via
    Cloudflare Pages, talking to Workers on api.droneinstitute.com.
  - Backend runtime: Workers Durable Objects (lightweight services) + Hyperdrive to PlanetScale for
    primary OLTP, with Prisma or Kysely for schema management.
  - Data storage: PlanetScale for normalized ERP model (orders, items, order_lines, customers,
    leads, integrations); S3 buckets (existing) for media and orthomosaics, with presigned URLs
    issued from Workers.
  - Auth: Move from Cognito to Cloudflare Access or WorkOS/Auth0 to unify B2B/B2C flows; issue JWTs
    consumed by Workers and frontends.
  - Event actions: use Cloudflare Queues + Workers Cron + Monday.com REST automation. Store
    workflow definitions in PlanetScale (JSON configs) to allow no-code toggles for ops.
  - Observability: Axiom for reactive dashboards/logs/traces; ingest Workers logs via wrangler
    bindings; wire TanStack Query devtools to surface user-side performance metrics.
  - Analytics: D

graphNodes.name: > The most generalizable skill out there is probably learnin
graphNodes.text_preview:
> The most generalizable skill out there is probably learning how to climb skill trees (and in particular, ones that are relentlessly hierarchical).
> Of course, you can't acquire that general skill without practicing it on particulars. You gotta lean into specific domains and acquire measurable, demonstrable skills.
> But once you see it, once you get a feeling for the process, you see it everywhere.


For software engineers, this shows up very clearly once you’ve climbed a few real skill trees.

Early on, everything feels flat. You “learn Java” or “learn backend” or “learn cloud” as if they’re single skills. But in reality, engineering skills are brutally hierarchical.

- You can’t reason about distributed systems if you don’t understand networking.
- You can’t design reliable services if you don’t understand failure modes.
- You can’t optimize performance if you don’t understand memory, caches, and data access patterns.

Once you recognize this, the strategy changes. You stop randomly collecting tools and start climbing deliberately. You pick a root (say backend engineering), then make sure each lower layer is solid before moving up: 

language internals → concurrency → I/O → databases → distributed coordination → system design.

Each layer unlocks the next. Skipping layers always comes back to hurt you.

The key is to practice on concrete problems. Don’t “study microservices” in the abstract. Build something small that breaks. Debug a production issue. Design a system that fails under load and fix it. Those experiences are the XP that move you up the tree.

Certifications and tutorials don’t level you up by themselves but solving real problems does.

After a while, you start seeing the same structure everywhere. Learning Rust, Go, or Java stops being scary because you know how to find the roots and work upward.

New domains become faster to enter because you recognize the hierarchy and where to start.

That’s when compounding kicks in. You’re no longer just lear

graphNodes.name: Claude Code is the purest Tool for Thought when equipped wit
graphNodes.text_preview:
Claude Code is the purest Tool for Thought when equipped with Skills to guide it's thinking. @rjs wrote a skillset that made explicit how he likes to think about problems. He's using CC to perform computation on his thoughts, with Opus as a constraint solver.

rjs:
"Seeing a weird inversion as I work more with AI to shape.

It used to be that the *concept* was mainly in my head. And then it was a huge lift at the end to make a *document* that spelled it all out as a source of truth for the project.

Now the concept -- what I have figured out to be true about both problem and solution -- is now stored as a ton of information in Markdown files maintained by the LLM. It's not in my head! And producing the document at the end to summarize it is a way faster step, because it's mostly generated!

Key to doing this is giving Claude Code skills for how to bin the information I'm giving it. The core is teaching it to untangle requirements (R) from solution ideas (S). It tracks these in tables. (This was easier than it sounds). Then I can do R x S and get a fit check (✅or❌against every R for each solution option in S: A, B, C...).

If there's something I don't know how to solve yet, but I need to get it out of my head, I just say "this needs to be solved" and Claude adds it to R and saves the Markdown. Claude is surprisingly good at seeing that the new R fails because nothing in any S or the current code resolves it.

Say I already have some solution A sketched out. But now I have a new idea for B. I can just say "new B ... same as A, but instead of managing calendars directly in the pane we'll just preview the calendars there and link out to the old management screen." It knows what I mean, summarizes B in a table of parts, even refers to the correct management screen (we're in the repo!) and updates the fit check (R x B) if we're now passing or failing requirements.

graphNodes.name: Prediction markets don't lie - they're systematically biased
graphNodes.text_preview:
Prediction markets don't lie - they're systematically biased

and that bias has geometry

we built a calibration surface C(K, τ) - a 2D error map across strike K and horizon τ

turns out: markets overprice extremes. underprice short horizons. drift optimistic on long ones

this isn't noise - it's structure

C(K, τ) = C_K(K) + C_τ(τ) + C_int

smile * temporal drift * interaction

three layers of systematic error you can measure, decompose, and correct

when MCI(τ) < 0.80 - the market loses price discovery

it's not about whether markets "believe" something - it's about whether they know what they don't know
